title: Booting Up: Load a Value
lesson:
Welcome, Longhorn. You found a humming machine in the basement of the ECE building: a 1976 MOS 6502, the 8-bit chip that powered the Apple I, the Commodore 64, and the NES. It cost $25 when rivals cost $200, which is why it wound up in just about everything.

The 6502 has three working registers: the accumulator A, plus two index registers X and Y. Almost everything starts by loading a number into one of them. LDA means 'LoaD the Accumulator'. The '#' means 'the literal number itself' (that's called immediate mode), and the '$' means hexadecimal.
prompt: Load the hex value $2A (that's 42 in decimal, the answer to everything) into the accumulator A. End your program with BRK so the machine stops.
starterCode:
; LDA #$xx loads a literal value into A.
; Change the value below, then press Run.
LDA #$00
BRK
hints: Immediate mode uses a '#'. For a hex value, write it as #$2A.
The whole program is just: LDA #$2A then BRK.
successText: The accumulator A must hold $2A when the program stops.
